extracted_information: The provided web content details Kubernetes multi-tenancy security models, namespace isolation, and security best practices. It outlines two primary implementation approaches: Namespace per tenant and Virtual Control Plane per tenant. The document emphasizes isolating tenants at both the control plane and data plane levels to ensure security, fairness, and resource management. It does not contain information specifically regarding implementation recommendations for cloud IDEs.

isolation_types: Control plane isolation: Ensures different tenants cannot access or affect each others' Kubernetes API resources.
Data plane isolation: Ensures pods and workloads for different tenants are sufficiently isolated.

multiple_teams.description: Sharing a cluster between multiple teams within an organization, where teams may have direct or indirect access to Kubernetes resources. Policies like RBAC, quotas, and network policies are essential.
multiple_teams.trust_level: Some level of trust between members.

multiple_customers.description: Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) vendor running multiple instances of a workload for customers. Customers do not have direct access to the cluster. Strong isolation and cost optimization are critical.

isolation_levels.hard_multi_tenancy: Implies strong isolation, often used when tenants do not trust each other (e.g., guarding against data exfiltration or DoS). Requires extra attention to data-plane isolation, control plane isolation remains critical.
isolation_levels.soft_multi_tenancy: Implies weaker isolation. 'Hardness' or 'softness' is a spectrum with various techniques for different isolation types.

features.name: Namespace Isolation
features.description: A mechanism for isolating groups of API resources within a single cluster. Allows object name overlap across namespaces and scopes many Kubernetes security policies (e.g., RBAC Roles, Network Policies) to namespaces. Common practice: isolate every workload in its own namespace. Requires configuration of other Kubernetes resources, networking plugins, and adherence to security best practices for proper isolation.

features.name: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
features.description: Used to enforce authorization in the Kubernetes control plane for both users and workloads (service accounts). Roles and RoleBindings are namespace-level objects. Critical to ensure tenants have appropriate access (Principle of Least Privilege). In multi-team environments, restricts tenant access to appropriate namespaces and ensures cluster-wide resources are accessed by privileged users only.

features.name: Resource Quotas
features.description: Manages resource usage (CPU, memory, API resources like Pods, ConfigMaps) of tenant workloads. Prevents a single tenant from monopolizing resources ('noisy neighbor' issue). Namespaced objects. Requires specifying resource requests and limits for each container. Cannot protect against all resource sharing (e.g., network traffic).

features.name: Network Policies
features.description: Controls pod-to-pod communication. Restricts communication using namespace labels or IP address ranges. Recommended: start with a default policy that denies communication between pods, then add permissive rules within a namespace. Does not apply to pods in different virtual control planes.

features.name: Service Meshes
features.description: Provides advanced network isolation with OSI Layer 7 policies based on workload identity, in addition to namespaces. Offers encryption using mutual TLS. More complex to manage.

features.name: Dynamic Volume Provisioning & StorageClasses
features.description: Recommended for storage security and data-isolation. StorageClasses describe custom storage 'classes'. PersistentVolumeClaim (namespaced) requests storage. PersistentVolume (cluster-wide) has an independent lifecycle. Shared StorageClasses should have a 'Delete' reclaim policy to prevent reuse across namespaces.

features.name: Sandboxing Containers
features.description: Provides stronger isolation than OS-level virtualization by running each pod in a separate execution environment (e.g., virtual machine or userspace kernel). Recommended for untrusted code to insulate the host from container escapes. Helps when universal seccomp, AppArmor, SELinux rules are hard to apply.

features.name: Node Isolation
features.description: Dedicates a set of nodes to run pods from a particular tenant, prohibiting co-mingling. Reduces 'noisy neighbor' and lowers information disclosure risk. Implemented using taints/tolerations, node selectors, and mutating webhooks. Kubelet and API service (unless virtual control planes) are still shared.

features.name: API Priority and Fairness
features.description: Allows assigning priority to pods. API calls from higher priority pods are fulfilled before lower priority ones. Lower priority calls can be queued or rejected.

features.name: Quality-of-Service (QoS)
features.description: Offers different service tiers using Kubernetes constructs. Includes: Network QoS (Kubernetes bandwidth plugin - experimental, uses requests/limits for rate limiting), Storage QoS (different StorageClasses for performance characteristics), Pod Priority and Preemption (assign priority values to pods, higher priority pods can evict lower priority ones).

features.name: DNS Configuration
features.description: By default, allows lookups across all namespaces. In multi-tenant environments, can be restricted to prevent cross-namespace lookups (e.g., CoreDNS leveraging Kubernetes metadata). For Virtual Control Plane per tenant, a per-tenant or multi-tenant DNS service is needed.

features.name: Operators
features.description: Kubernetes controllers that manage applications. In multi-tenant environments, Operators should support creating resources within different tenant namespaces, configure Pods with resource requests and limits, and support data-plane isolation techniques (node isolation, sandboxed containers).
